context: In a matter of weeks the market has collapsed about a third. Cities are locking down, nobody knows how bad it gets, and serious people are saying 'depression'. You hold $7,000 in stocks — now worth far less — and $3,000 in cash on the side.
question: It's still falling. What do you do?

choices.label: Sell everything
choices.sub: Cut the loss. Go to cash.

_feed_note: Real market news (event-anonymous to keep it blind) + community posts FAITHFULLY RECREATED as representative period sentiment (handles are fictional archetypes, not real people).

feed.text: Stocks open the year at record highs. Strategists see clear skies ahead.
This bull market turns 11 next year. Just keep buying. It only goes up.
First red week in forever. Healthy pullback. Loading up here.
Stocks tumble in their worst day in two years.
Down ~8% from the highs in days. Anyone else starting to sweat?
Worst week for the market since the 2008 financial crisis.
Trading halted at the open as stocks plunge — circuit breaker triggered.
A circuit breaker. I've never seen this. Down 20% from my peak. Can't sleep.
Markets in free fall — second trading halt of the week.
Just sold everything. Cash is the only safe place. This is 2008 times ten.
Largest single-day point drop in history.
My retirement account is down a third. I can't watch this anymore.
They're going to shut the whole thing down. Zero is on the table. Get out while you can.
Everyone I know just sold. That's usually the bottom. Be greedy when others are fearful.
I give up. Going to cash and never touching the market again.

reveal.headline: March 2020. The COVID crash.
facts: The S&P 500 fell about 34% in 33 days — the fastest bear market in history.
It bottomed days after this moment, then ripped to a new all-time high by August 2020.
Selling here locked in the loss. Holding recovered everything. Buying the fear was the trade of the decade — but no one knew that standing where you stood.
